topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the locker room felt thick, suffocating. Liam stared at his worn sneakers, the familiar leather suddenly feeling like a lead weight on his feet. He had poured every ounce of his being into those grueling practices, the early mornings, the relentless conditioning, all for this moment. Now, the coach’s announcement echoed in the silence, a verdict delivered with practiced, impersonal calm. He didn't move. His jaw tightened so hard it ached. When the coach finally walked past, Liam didn't bother to look up. He just pulled his damp jersey tighter around himself, the fabric clinging uncomfortably to his skin. Every muscle in his body felt heavy, unresponsive, as if gravity had suddenly tripled its pull. He shuffled toward the exit, his steps dragging, each movement an effort against an invisible resistance.

[story 2]

"So, that's it then." The words hung in the sterile hallway, sharp and cold. Maya felt a strange hollowness bloom in her chest, a sensation like a punctured lung. She watched the other hopefuls gather their gear, their laughter bright and effortless, a sound that grated on her nerves. Her hands were clammy, slick with sweat that had nothing to do with the exertion of practice. She kept her gaze fixed on the scuff marks on the polished floor, tracing them with her eyes as if the pattern held some secret reassurance. When the team captain offered a sympathetic nod, Maya managed a thin, brittle smile that didn't reach her eyes. She nodded back, the motion stiff, and turned to walk away, the sound of her own footsteps echoing too loudly in the empty space.

[story 3]

I sat on the edge of the bench, the cold metal biting through my thin shorts. The silence after the announcement was deafening, amplifying the frantic drumming of my own heart against my ribs. I picked at a loose thread on my shorts, the rough texture a small, grounding distraction from the churning in my stomach. Every memory of the early mornings, the sweat stinging my eyes, felt suddenly distant, like watching a film of someone else's life. I looked around the room, at the confident postures of the players who had earned their spot, and a profound sense of being utterly misplaced washed over me. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I didn't want to speak; the simple act of forming words felt too exhausting. I just wanted the floor to swallow me whole.
